front: Manuscript: the largest surviving collection of Anglo-Saxon verse
Manuscript: one of four major codices of Old English literature; the others are the Vercelli Book, the Nowell Codex (containing Beowulf), and the Caedmon manuscript
Manuscript: contains over 90 riddles with double-entendre content; a key and an onion are cited examples
Manuscript: contains four major poems in the ubi sunt genre
Manuscript: contains three allegorical beast poems — 'The Phoenix', 'The Panther', and one describing the Fastitocalon
Poem: unfinished Old English elegy containing the line 'the work of giants is decaying'
Poem: 'master artisans' lie buried for 'a hundred generations' in this Old English elegy
Poem: Old English words from it — including enta and orþonc — inspired character and place names in Tolkien's work
Poem: translated in 1911 by Ezra Pound; ends with the single word 'Amen'
Poem: narrator says 'Blithe heart cannot know... what hardships they suffer / who drive the foam-furrow furthest from land'
Poem: contains the passage predicting that 'those doomed to die' will be killed by the 'sword's hatred'; the narrator notes 'I do not believe that the riches of the world will stand forever'
Poem: the narrator recalls how the 'skilful poet' Heorrenda received lands promised to him; the refrain is 'that passed away, so may this'
Poem: 'The Wife's Lament' and 'The Husband's Message' are both examples of this Old English genre found in the Exeter Book
back: The Wife's Lament / The Husband's Message (Exeter Book)
work: The Wife's Lament / The Husband's Message

front: Poem: contains the line 'Hail Earendel brightest of Angels / over Middle Earth sent to men,' which inspired Tolkien's name for his fictional world
